pair.title: Alberta startup sells no-tech tractors for half price

story.title: This Alberta Startup Sells No-Tech Tractors for Half Price

comments.text: This is exactly what the right-to-repair movement needs. Half the price of a new Deere with engines that farmers can actually service themselves instead of calling an authorized dealer. The 400 US inquiries after one interview says everything about farmer frustration with locked-down equipment. Hope they can scale production without getting sued into oblivion.

kids.text: My uncle runs a 500-acre grain operation in Saskatchewan and spent $8k last year on dealer service calls because his 2019 Deere had a hydraulic fault that threw a software error. The dealer wanted $3,200 just to diagnose it. He ended up paying a retired mechanic from two towns over $400 in cash to physically swap the sensor, but had to have the dealer re-flash the firmware anyway. A tractor like this Ursa would've paid for itself in avoided locksmith fees within three seasons. The real question is whether they can source enough remanufactured Cummins blocks without getting choked out by supply issues. Those engines are rock solid but they're not exactly sitting around unused anymore.

I had a similar situation with a 2015 Case IH. Transmission threw a code and the dealer quoted $5,400 for parts and labor. Turns out it was just a $40 solenoid, but the diagnostic fee alone was $1,200 because they had to "interface with the onboard system." Finally found a retired mechanic who could read the old Case manuals without needing a dealership login. Cost me $300 total. That was five years ago and I've thought about it every time I see another repair bill. Ursa's timing is perfect.

Same thing happened to my neighbor with a 2018 Kubota. The dealer wanted $2,100 just to clear a fuel filter warning code. Took it to a small-engine shop in town, $85 and a phone call to someone who actually understood the old Kubota manuals. Deere's playbook copied that entire model.

I'd push back gently on the "farmers can service themselves" framing. Cummins 5.9L and 8.3L P-pump engines are mechanically robust, sure, but modern ag work still requires diagnostic tooling most farmers don't have. The real win here is that Ursa's using engines with 30+ years of aftermarket support and parts availability that aren't locked behind a dealer network. You can source a fuel pump rebuild kit from any diesel shop, not wait 6 weeks for a John Deere ECU reflash. That's the actual leverage point against the locked-down ecosystem.

comments.text: The Cummins 5.9 was genuinely bulletproof, but I'd want to see the actual warranty terms here. A remanufactured engine from the 90s is still 30+ years old-parts availability for fuel pumps, injectors, and block work gets thinner every year. Ursa's betting on simplicity (which helps), but the real question is whether they're backing these with 5-year powertrain coverage or if it's caveat emptor. That changes the actual cost of ownership calculus pretty dramatically versus a new Deere with dealer support.

kids.text: I've been running a small fleet of older equipment on my farm for fifteen years, and this is exactly where the regulatory capture stings hardest. Right-to-repair should've been settled law by now, but John Deere's lobbying keeps it locked down. Ursa's betting farmers will choose simplicity over the dealer network, and honestly, at half price with mechanical injection, they've got a real shot. Warranty's the blind spot though.

comments.text: Interesting timing. There's a UK outfit, Agri-X, doing something similar with older Case IH powerplants and zero software locks. They're shipping about 80 units/year to EU farmers at comparable pricing. The real test will be whether Ursa can scale parts supply and warranty infrastructure across the border without getting sued by Deere's IP team. That said, if they can hit 500+ units annually, they've got a sustainable business model that John Deere's margins can't touch.

kids.text: There's also AGCO's experiment with simpler mechanicals through Massey Ferguson's heritage line, though they're still bundling some onboard diagnostics. The real difference with Ursa and Agri-X is they're betting farmers will accept a ten-year-old architecture if it means no DRM on the fuel injector. Deere's legal team will absolutely notice once Ursa hits $20M revenue though.

The Bosch P-pump detail is key here. Mechanical injection means a farmer with basic tools and a manual can diagnose fuel delivery without a $500 diagnostic scanner or a dealer appointment. That's the real value proposition, not just the price tag. Deere's margins depend on keeping the service pipeline exclusive. Once you've got 500 units in the field running trouble-free for five years, the comparison becomes brutal for their bottom line.

comments.text: Worth noting Deere's own used-equipment market has gotten brutal for them because of this. Farmers are actively buying 15-20 year old iron at a premium over new, specifically to avoid the software lock-in. Ursa's timing is sharp but they're really just filling a void Deere created.

kids.text: The economics here are brutal for Deere once you do the math. A used 2005 Deere 7720 with 6000 hours runs $80-90k now. A new equivalent would be $250k+, but with mandatory dealer service, proprietary parts, and firmware updates that disable features if you skip a recall. Farmers aren't being romantic about old iron-they're doing cost-benefit analysis. Ursa's $95k entry point directly undercuts that entire used market segment while offering mechanical simplicity. The real tell is Deere's response: they've been aggressive on warranty denial for farmers who tinker, which just accelerates the flight to anything without an ECU.

I remember when farmers could actually fix their own equipment. My uncle had a 1970s Massey Ferguson that ran for thirty years on parts he sourced himself and a manual. Now you need dealer certification just to change the oil on a new Deere. That said, I'm skeptical Ursa scales beyond niche demand. Remanufactured Cummins engines are solid, but what happens when one fails at harvest? Do they have parts inventory and dealer support across the US, or are farmers trading software lock-in for supply-chain fragility?

You've nailed it. The used-equipment premium is the real market signal we couldn't ignore. We're not inventing demand here-we're just removing the friction that pushed farmers toward decade-old machines in the first place. The fact that a 2004 Deere commands more than a 2024 one says everything about where the actual pain lives.
